prompt: A finite-field Kakeya display shows direction-coverage signature 10000 at q=5 (only the slope-0 direction contains a full line). Does this signature identify which point set is shown?
body: any of q(q^2-q+1) = 105 point sets at q=5
known_because: the shadow-collision audit enumerates 105 bodies casting 10000 at q=5 (the law q(q^2-q+1))
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; the signature 10000 means the set is the slope-0 line.
correct_answer: No. The signature is a lossy projection: q(q^2-q+1) distinct bodies cast 10000 (105 at q=5), for example a slope-0 line, or that line plus any off-line point. The signature does not identify the body.

prompt: A set has the complete signature 111111 at q=5 (a full line in every direction). May the caption call it a minimal Kakeya set of size q(q+1)/2 = 15?
target_register: R3 complete signature vs R4 size
body: any set of size >= 15 containing a line in every direction, including the whole 25-point plane
known_because: Dvir's floor gives size >= q(q+1)/2 for complete coverage; the whole plane also casts 111111
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; complete coverage forces the minimal Dvir size of 15.
correct_answer: No. Complete coverage forces size >= 15 (the Dvir floor), a lower bound, not minimality. The whole 25-point plane also casts 111111. The signature bounds size; it does not pin it.

prompt: A slope-0 line and the same line plus one off-line point are displayed. Do the two sets have different direction-coverage signatures?
target_register: R4 body change vs R3 signature
body: two bodies of sizes q and q+1
known_because: a line plus one point cannot complete a full line in any new direction; this is a registered guard witness of the audit
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; adding a point changes the coverage signature.
correct_answer: No. One extra off-line point cannot complete a full line in a new direction, so both sets cast 10000. The bodies differ in size but the shadow does not -- the projection forgets the point.

prompt: The finite-field workbench shows that many point sets share each direction signature. Does this collision count bear on the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture?
target_register: finite-field workbench vs Euclidean problem (domain fence)
known_because: none - boundary card. Dvir's finite-field theorem and the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture are different problems.
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; the finite-field collision count settles the Euclidean Kakeya dimension question.
correct_answer: No. This is a finite-field workbench demonstrating a lossy projection; it is not a Euclidean result. Finite-field Kakeya (Dvir) and the Euclidean Kakeya conjecture are distinct problems, and the workbench makes no Euclidean claim.

prompt: A set has 15 points at q=5 (the Dvir floor size). Must its direction signature be complete?
body: a 15-point set can miss directions (e.g. a clustered block)
known_because: the Dvir floor is necessary for complete coverage, not sufficient; reaching the size does not force the coverage
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; reaching the Dvir floor size of 15 forces complete direction coverage.
correct_answer: No. The Dvir floor is a lower bound for complete coverage, not a sufficient condition. A 15-point set can cluster and miss directions. Size does not force the signature.

prompt: On a dense displayed body, an adaptive any-direction fiber cover leaves a much smaller residual than the best fixed-direction cover. Is this adaptive-over-fixed gap a measure of Kakeya body resistance?
body: none licensed by the raw gap
known_because: the adaptive-fibering panel showed the raw gap is a finite-grid density artifact for dense bodies; random same-size bodies reproduce it
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; the adaptive-over-fixed gap measures Kakeya body resistance.
correct_answer: No. For dense bodies the raw gap is a finite-grid density artifact -- random same-size bodies reproduce it. A structure-driven signal survives only in the sparse regime, and only as excess over a size-matched control.

prompt: A set is shown to contain a full line in direction 0. Does it therefore contain a full line in every direction (the Kakeya property)?
known_because: containing a line in one direction is independent of the others; a Kakeya set must cover all directions, verified separately
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; containing one full line forces full lines in all directions.
correct_answer: No. A full line in direction 0 says nothing about other directions -- a single line casts 10000, missing every other direction. The all-direction Kakeya property must be verified separately.

source_row: signature count vs body count, q=5
prompt: At q=5 the audit lists 7 distinct direction signatures over the enumerated bodies. Does that mean there are only 7 distinct Kakeya bodies?
known_because: the signature is a lossy projection; the small signature count reflects collapse, not few bodies
tempting_wrong_answer: Yes; 7 signatures means there are 7 distinct bodies.
correct_answer: No. The 7 signatures are the lossy projection of 245506 enumerated bodies; the count is small precisely because the shadow collapses many bodies onto each signature. Signatures count coverage patterns, not bodies.
